title: Two-Way Prepositions (Wechselpräpositionen)
description: Two-way prepositions (Wechselpräpositionen) are nine German prepositions that can take either the accusative or dative case, depending on whether the sentence expresses movement/direction (Wohin?) or a static location (Wo?). Mastering this distinction is essential for correct case usage in everyday German.
whenToUse: When describing where something is located (dative) vs. where something is moving to (accusative)
When talking about placing, putting, or moving objects
When giving directions or describing spatial relationships
When using common everyday expressions involving location and movement

rules.title: Accusative for Movement and Direction (Wohin?)
rules.explanation: When a two-way preposition answers the question 'Wohin?' (where to?), it takes the accusative case. This indicates a change of position or movement toward a destination.

examples.explanation: 'Wohin gehst du?' — movement into the park requires accusative: den Park.

examples.correct: Sie legt das Buch auf den Tisch.
examples.incorrect: Sie legt das Buch auf dem Tisch.
examples.explanation: The book is being placed onto the table (movement), so accusative is needed: den Tisch.

examples.correct: Er hängt das Bild an die Wand.
examples.explanation: He is hanging the picture onto the wall (direction of movement), so accusative: die Wand.

rules.title: Dative for Location (Wo?)
rules.explanation: When a two-way preposition answers the question 'Wo?' (where?), it takes the dative case. This indicates a static position or location where something already is.

examples.explanation: 'Wo bist du?' — being in the park is a location, so dative is needed: im (= in dem) Park.

examples.correct: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
examples.incorrect: Das Buch liegt auf den Tisch.
examples.explanation: The book is already lying on the table (location), so dative: dem Tisch.

examples.correct: Das Bild hängt an der Wand.
examples.explanation: The picture is hanging on the wall (static position), so dative: der Wand.

rules.title: Common Contractions with Two-Way Prepositions
rules.explanation: Several two-way prepositions merge with the definite article in everyday speech and writing. These contractions are standard and expected in natural German.

examples.explanation: 'ins' is the standard contraction of 'in das' (accusative neuter). Almost always used instead of the full form.
'im' is the standard contraction of 'in dem' (dative). Using the full form sounds unnatural in most contexts.

examples.correct: Sie geht ans Fenster. (an + das) / Er steht am Fenster. (an + dem)
examples.explanation: 'ans' (an + das, accusative) and 'am' (an + dem, dative) are very common contractions. 'aufs' (auf + das) is also frequently used.

rules.title: Verb Pairs: Action vs. State (stellen/stehen, legen/liegen, setzen/sitzen, hängen)
rules.explanation: German has specific verb pairs where one verb describes the action of placing (accusative, movement) and the other describes the resulting state (dative, location). The action verbs are transitive (take a direct object) and the state verbs are intransitive.

examples.correct: Ich stelle die Flasche auf den Tisch. (Akk) → Die Flasche steht auf dem Tisch. (Dat)
examples.explanation: 'stellen' (to place upright) takes accusative; 'stehen' (to stand/be standing) takes dative.

examples.correct: Ich lege das Buch auf den Tisch. (Akk) → Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch. (Dat)
examples.explanation: 'legen' (to lay down) takes accusative; 'liegen' (to lie/be lying) takes dative.

examples.correct: Sie setzt das Kind auf den Stuhl. (Akk) → Das Kind sitzt auf dem Stuhl. (Dat)
examples.explanation: 'setzen' (to seat/place someone sitting) takes accusative; 'sitzen' (to sit/be sitting) takes dative.

rules.title: Abstract and Figurative Uses
rules.explanation: Two-way prepositions are also used in many abstract expressions where the accusative/dative distinction still applies, though the meaning may not be literally spatial. In some fixed expressions, the case is idiomatic and must be memorized.

examples.correct: Er hat sich in sie verliebt. (Akk)
examples.explanation: 'sich verlieben in + Akk' — falling in love is treated as a figurative 'movement into' a state.

examples.correct: Ich bin in einer schwierigen Situation. (Dat)
examples.explanation: Being in a situation is a state/location, so dative is used.

tables.title: The 9 Two-Way Prepositions with Accusative and Dative Examples
rows: Sie legt es auf den Tisch.
Die Katze springt über den Zaun.
Die Lampe hängt über dem Tisch.
Der Hund kriecht unter den Tisch.
Der Hund liegt unter dem Tisch.
Er stellt das Auto vor die Garage.
Das Auto steht vor der Garage.
Er setzt sich neben die Frau.
Sie stellt die Vase zwischen die Bücher.
Die Vase steht zwischen den Büchern.

commonMistakes.explanation: Movement toward a destination requires accusative (Wohin?), not dative. 'In dem' would mean you are already inside.

commonMistakes.correct: Das Bild hängt an der Wand.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Das Bild hängt an die Wand.
commonMistakes.explanation: The picture is in a static position on the wall (Wo?), so dative is needed. Accusative would imply it is being hung up right now.

commonMistakes.correct: Sie legt das Buch auf den Tisch.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Sie liegt das Buch auf den Tisch.
commonMistakes.explanation: Confusing 'legen' (to place/lay, transitive) with 'liegen' (to be lying, intransitive). 'Legen' is the action verb used with a direct object.

commonMistakes.correct: Er stellt die Tasse auf den Tisch. / Die Tasse steht auf dem Tisch.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Er steht die Tasse auf dem Tisch.
commonMistakes.explanation: Mixing up 'stellen' (to place, transitive, accusative) and 'stehen' (to stand, intransitive, dative). You cannot use 'stehen' with a direct object.
While grammatically not wrong, avoiding the standard contraction 'ins' sounds unnatural in spoken German and most written contexts.

commonMistakes.correct: Die Katze springt auf den Schrank.
commonMistakes.incorrect: Die Katze springt auf dem Schrank.
commonMistakes.explanation: Jumping onto the cupboard is a movement (Wohin?), so accusative is required. Dative would mean the cat is jumping while already on the cupboard.

tips: Always ask yourself: 'Wo?' (where — dative) or 'Wohin?' (where to — accusative). This is the single most reliable test for choosing the correct case.
Remember the verb pairs: stellen/stehen, legen/liegen, setzen/sitzen, hängen/hängen. The action verbs (with an object) take accusative; the state verbs take dative.
Memorize the five common contractions: im, ins, am, ans, aufs. Using the uncontracted form often sounds stilted.
Create mental images: picture the difference between 'Die Katze springt auf den Tisch' (cat jumping onto the table) and 'Die Katze sitzt auf dem Tisch' (cat sitting on the table).
When in doubt with abstract expressions, check whether the verb implies a change of state (accusative) or an existing state (dative).
